title: Improve the Circt build system
body:
I wanted to start a discussion about how we can improve the circt build system, please let me know if there is a better venue than this issue.

During the (admittedly brief) time I've been working with Circt, I've experienced pain and seen others experience pain related to the build system. Some concrete examples include: the Xcode generator not working, a two-step build process, binary artifacts not being installed. My gut feeling for why this is the case is that Circt lives with one foot in and one foot out of the LLVM build system. @stephenneuendorffer opined [here](https://github.com/llvm/circt/issues/201) that we could improve this by moving further away from LLVM, but I feel like LLVM has reasonable solutions for a bunch of stuff in the CMake world and moving away risks us reinventing the wheel and making a potential future reconciliation with LLVM/MLIR more difficult. 

My alternative suggestion is that we buy into the LLVM system _more_, not less. In this world, Circt is would just be a fork of LLVM with a new "circt" sub project which in the file system is a sibling to `llvm` and `mlir`. The main drawback to this approach is that we no longer have a clear separation between LLVM commits (which currently end up in the `circt/llvm` fork) and `circt` commits, but I think we can effectively mitigate this by creating a GitHub action that only passes for commits that are either (1) entirely additive in directories we have "blessed" as being part of circt (2) do not touch _any_ circt files or folders, and thus can eventually be upstreamed to LLVM verbatim. 

The benefits of this approach are that (hopefully) having llvm drive the build will give us all the goodness that llvm provides without having too roll our own solutions or debug partially-adopted llvm solutions. The build also becomes single-stage at this point so a simple 
```
$ cd circt
$ mkdir llvm/build
$ cd llvm/build
$ cmake -G Ninja ../llvm \
    -DLLVM_ENABLE_PROJECTS="circt" \
    -DLLVM_TARGETS_TO_BUILD="X86;RISCV" \
    -DLLVM_ENABLE_ASSERTIONS=ON \
    -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=DEBUG
$ ninja
$ ninja check-circt
```
will do the right thing. One other consequence of having a single build step is that you could work with MLIR and Circt in a single workspace in IDEs that support cmake integration (like VSCode).

There are also no submodules to manage with this approach, updating the submodule would be replaced by simply merging new commits from the upstream LLVM. Since the circt commits are required to only be additive there should be no LLVM-circt merge conflicts. This will also get rid of the weirdness of having `llvm/circt` depend on `circt/llvm` (I'd support the `circt/llvm` fork simply becoming the new repository, though we can also manually make `llvm/circt` a fork of `llvm`), and make [this](https://github.com/llvm/circt/pull/188) a non-issue.

All that said, I understand that this may be disruptive to `circt`'s community of contributors and we should weight the impact of that versus the benefits of simplifying the build.
